icpRubric.notes: How the **target company** makes money (revenue / commercial motion). Distinct from `product.delivery_model`. Implement `icp-dimensions.ts` from `values` below.

description: How the target company makes money — revenue model and commercial motion.
sourceNote: Content derived from the prior markdown brief on common startup business models.

values.label: SaaS (Software as a Service)
values.definition: Cloud-based subscription software sold to businesses, typically measured on MRR/ARR, NRR, and CAC.
primary_metrics: Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR) or Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR)
Growth rate (weekly or monthly)
Net Revenue Retention: % of recurring revenue retained from a prior period
CAC: costs to acquire a new customer
takeaways: Recurring revenue profile
Non-recurring revenue may exist but is usually excluded from ARR/MRR
Usually B2B; annual contracts are common
Growth via direct sales, self-serve, or both

values.label: Transactional
values.tagline: Facilitate transactions and take a cut
values.definition: Facilitates transactions and earns fees (often a small percentage of volume); common in fintech and payments.
primary_metrics: Gross Transaction Value (GTV): total payment volume transacted
Net revenue: fees charged for transactions (often a %)
User retention: % of month-1 customers who purchase again in month 2, etc.
CAC
takeaways: Often fintech and payments
Transaction-oriented rather than classic subscription
High volume, low take rate (e.g. 1–3%) is common
Best operators show consistent revenue from repeat usage

values.label: Marketplaces
values.tagline: Facilitate transactions between buyers and sellers
values.definition: Connects buyers and sellers; revenue usually from GMV-based fees or take rate.
primary_metrics: Gross Merchandise Value (GMV): total sales volume transacted
Net revenue: fees (often a % take rate)
Growth rate
User retention: % of month-1 customers who purchase again in month 2, etc.
takeaways: Cold start / chicken-and-egg
Supply and demand must scale together
Network effects can drive winner-take-most outcomes

values.label: Subscription
values.tagline: Recurring product or service, often consumer
values.definition: Product or service sold on a recurring basis, usually to consumers at lower price points and higher volume.
primary_metrics: MRR or ARR
Growth rate (weekly or monthly)
User retention: % of month-1 customers who purchase again in month 2, etc.
CAC
takeaways: Recurring revenue is highly valued
Often consumer, monthly billing
Lower price points, higher customer counts
Growth often via scalable self-serve acquisition

values.label: Enterprise
values.tagline: Large fixed-term contracts to big companies
values.definition: Large contracts to enterprises (e.g. 5k+ employees cited in source material); bookings, ACV, and pipeline-heavy.
primary_metrics: Bookings: total signed contract value (recurring + non-recurring)
Revenue: recognized when delivering on the contract
Annual Contract Value (ACV): total contract value / number of years
Pipeline: top of funnel → demo → close
takeaways: Few customers, large deals (e.g. $100k+/year)
Direct sales–driven
Paid pilots or LOIs are common entry paths
Long cycles, many stakeholders; buyer may differ from end user
Growth can be lumpy vs smooth MRR SaaS

values.label: Usage based
values.definition: Revenue scales with consumption (API calls, records, data, etc.) in a period; not the same as subscription MRR.
primary_metrics: Monthly revenue (not classic recurring)
Growth rate
Revenue retention: % of revenue from last month’s customers in this month
Gross margin: revenue minus COGS
takeaways: Do not conflate with subscription recurring revenue
Pricing tied to usage units
Revenue grows as customers grow
Product and pricing span small startups to large enterprises

values.label: E-commerce
values.definition: Sells goods online (including D2C and branded stores); keeps full sale margin vs marketplace model.
primary_metrics: Monthly revenue: total sales
Growth rate (weekly or monthly)
Gross margin / unit economics: revenue minus COGS
CAC
takeaways: Includes D2C and Shopify-style stores
Not a marketplace: typically 100% of each sale to the merchant
Higher COGS implies lower margins
Often commoditized categories; strength in acquisition and operations

values.label: Advertising
values.definition: Ad-supported model; advertisers pay for reach; scale and engagement metrics dominate.
primary_metrics: Daily Active Users (DAU): unique users in a 24-hour period
Monthly Active Users (MAU): unique users in a 28-day period
User retention: % of active users on D1/7/30/etc.
CPM (cost per thousand) or CPC (cost per click)
takeaways: Often large-scale consumer surfaces
Customer is the advertiser; users are the audience
Requires very large impression volume
Registered users alone can be a vanity metric

values.label: Hardtech / bio / moonshots
values.definition: Heavy technical and/or regulatory path; revenue may be far out; milestones, contracts, and LOIs matter early.
primary_metrics: Milestones: progress toward long-term vision
Signed contracts
Letters of Intent (LOIs): non-binding interest to purchase
takeaways: Years to live product due to technical and/or regulatory risk
Technical or clinical milestones can de-risk
Revenue often distant; LOIs can signal demand
